risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no), to either (a) immediate instruction in the TM program or (b) wait list/delayed start condition." Comment: The study explicitly describes a random blocks method with stratification, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence rather than a predictable or non-random rule.

_evidence.method_quote: Students were then randomized to experimental or control groups, using the random blocks method21 stratifying on gender (male/female) and self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no), to either (a) immediate instruction in the TM program or (b) wait list/delayed start condition.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were assigned to groups using random blocks with stratification by gender and ADHD status.
context_clues: self-reported attention deficit hyperactivity disorder status (yes, no)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing." Comment: This is an explicit concealment mechanism that prevents the recruiter/project manager from knowing the next assignment before enrollment/baseline completion, so foreknowledge of allocation was not possible.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The treatment group allocations were concealed by the study statistician, with individual treatment group assignments revealed to the project manager only when study participants completed baseline testing.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls" and "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: The article explicitly states that the study was single-blind and only the assessors were masked; participants and intervention personnel were not blinded. Because the trial reported subjective outcomes (psychological distress, anxiety, depression, anger/hostility, coping ability) that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, this domain is at high risk of performance bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls on change in BP, psychological distress, and coping in young adults. ... All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design.
_evidence.blinding_method: wait-list controls; assessments by research staff who were masked to treatment condition; single-blind design

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded/masked, and the study used objective BP measurement plus self-report psychological outcomes collected by masked staff. Because the assessor was blinded, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement of either the objective BP outcome or the questionnaire-based outcomes in this trial.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: This was a single-blind RCT comparing stress reduction with the TM program to wait-list controls on change in BP, psychological distress, and coping in young adults. ... All assessments were administered by research staff who were masked to treatment condition. Thus, this was a single-blind design.

outcomes.label: psychological distress (total mood disturbance, anxiety, depression, anger/hostility)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A limitation is that about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing." Comment: The paper reports substantial overall attrition, but it does not provide per-arm randomized denominators or a per-arm differential loss for the analysed outcomes. Analyses were complete-case on the 207 subjects with baseline and post-test data, yet the reported information is insufficient to determine whether missing outcome data were balanced across groups or likely to bias the results. The statement that completers did not differ from non-completers at baseline is reassuring, but without arm-specific attrition numbers and reasons for loss on the analysed outcome, the risk of bias remains unclear rather than definitively high or low.

_evidence.notes: Primary analyses were reported for 'the 207 subjects who completed baseline and post-test' and 'all eligible subjects with baseline and post-test data'; no per-arm randomised numbers were reported. The article states 'about 30% of the pretest sample was unavailable for post-testing' and '91' did not complete post-testing.
